segments.s418-g97: This clause strings two past actions without a conjunction, a common narrative device: بیامد (past third-person) 'he came' followed by نشست (here the verb 'sat') describing what he did on arrival. بران is the fused prepositional phrase بر آن 'upon that' placed before the noun phrase کرسی زر 'golden throne' (زر postmodifies کرسی), so the whole phrase reads 'he came and sat upon that golden throne.' The subject is omitted and understood from the verbs (3rd person).
segments.s418-g98: پر از خشم is an adjectival phrase 'full of anger' formed with پر + از + noun (پر 'full (of)' + از 'of'). بویا is an archaic past copula 'was' (3rd person) linking this predicate to the (omitted) subject from the previous clause. ترنجی بدست is a small noun phrase 'a roundel in (his) hand' where بدست functions as a locative/possessive 'in the hand' (i.e., 'holding'). Put together: 'full of anger, he was, a roundel in his hand' → 'full of anger, he held a roundel in his hand.'
segments.s418-g65: بخندید is a past tense verb (he/it laughed) placed at the start for narrative effect; here it takes از + name to mark the target of ridicule, so «بخندید از رستم اسفندیار» means “Esfandiyar laughed at Rostam,” with the agent name (اسفندیار) placed after the object. The use of از with خندیدن is an older/poetic pattern meaning “to laugh at/against.”
segments.s418-g66: بدو گفت is literally “to him said”: بدو (to him) precedes گفت (said), a common order in classical Persian where the indirect object can come before the verb. کای is a vocative particle “O,” followed by پور سام (an epithet: “son/descendant of Sam”) and سوار (“rider,” an appositive). Note: گفت is used as the past-tense reporting verb “said.”
segments.s418-g67: شدی is the past second‑person form “you became,” so the speaker addresses someone directly. تنگدل is an adjectival compound ‘narrow‑hearted / sullen.’ چون here introduces the subordinate clause meaning “when / because,” and نیامد is simple past “he/it did not come.” خرام is a noun/verb noun meaning “prancing” or “one who strides proudly” — together, the clause reads “you became sorrowful when the proud strider did not come.”
segments.s418-g68: نجستم here means “I sought” or (in the negative sense of the context) “I did not seek” — the speaker denies pursuing something. همی is an archaic/emphatic particle common in Ferdowsi (here reinforcing the verb). زین is the poetic contraction of از این (“from this”) introducing the source (these words). سخن is “speech/words,” and کام و نام form a paired object meaning “desire/pleasure and name/reputation.” The whole clause: “I did not seek from these words desire or renown.”
segments.s418-g69: This is a nominal phrase describing the hardship: چنان/چنین is an emphatic demonstrative meaning “such,” modifying the following nouns. گرم بد روز و راه دراز is a coordinate noun phrase (‘hot/bitter [or severe] day and a long road’) with گرم and بد qualifying روز, and راه followed by the adjective دراز. The word order is poetic but expected: modifier (چنین) + adjectives + noun + conjunction + noun + adjective.
segments.s418-g70: نکردم is the 1st-person singular past (negated) of کردن: “I did not make/cause.” The sequence ت + را represents the enclitic object pronoun -ت (ترا = ‘you’) followed by the object marker را (emphatic/definite). رنجه is the noun ‘suffering/distress’ that is the object of کردن. تندی مساز is an admonition: مساز is the prohibitive/negative imperative ‘do not make’ and تندی (‘haste’/‘harshness’) is its object, so ‘do not be quick/harsh (in causing distress).’ Together the clause says the speaker didn’t cause you the suffering and then implores the addressee not to act hastily/harshly.
segments.s418-g71: همی before a past tense verb gives a continuous or iterative sense in Classical/Persian poetry: همی گفتم = ‘I kept saying’ / ‘I was saying repeatedly.’ از بامداد پگاه is a temporal prepositional phrase ‘from dawn/early morning’; the two dawn-words are a poetic doubling for emphasis.
segments.s418-g72: به + پوزش introduces the means/subject-matter ‘with / in (a) pardon/apology.’ بسازم is 1st-person present-subjunctive/optative of ساختن/ساختن used to express the speaker’s intended action reported after گفتم (‘I said I would make/prepare’). سوی داد راه is a directional phrase: سوی = ‘toward,’ داد = ‘justice,’ راه = ‘path/road’ — together ‘toward the path of justice.’ The clause thus reports a spoken intention: ‘I kept saying I would (by way of) apology make my way toward justice.’
segments.s418-g17: بر + این + سان (written here as برین سان) is a set phrase meaning “in this manner; thus.” همی is an older/prosodic prefix indicating ongoing or habitual action (here: ‘ever’ or ‘continually’). از پی تاج is a prepositional phrase 'in pursuit of the crown' (از پی = 'after/in pursuit of', تاج = 'crown'), and و گاه adds 'and sometimes' (گه = 'occasion/occasionally'). Together this segment sets the manner and motive for the action that follows.
segments.s418-g18: به کشتن دهد is a causative expression: دادن + کشتن = 'to cause to be killed / to hand over to death.' The verb دهد is present/past-archaic form meaning 'gives/causes'. نامداری is the patient/affected noun 'the renowned one' and چو ماه is a simile 'like the moon' modifying that noun. Word order is verb before its noun phrase in this poetic inversion, with چو introducing the simile after the noun.
segments.s418-g19: به پیری is a temporal phrase 'in old age.' سوی + noun (سوی گنج) means 'toward the treasure.' یازان here functions as an adjectival/participle-like word 'open/turned toward' and ترست is a comparative-state predicate 'is more' (archaic comparative form). Overall: 'In old age he is more turned toward treasure.'
segments.s418-g20: This parallel clause has two prepositional phrases joined by و: به مهر 'toward love' and به دیهیم نازان 'toward rustic/coquettish maidens.' نازان is an adjective/noun 'coquettish/darlings' and ترست again is the comparative predicate 'is more.' The structure repeats the pattern of degree set up in the previous segment.
segments.s418-g21: همی آمد is the customary poetic progressive/past 'came' (همی + آمد). از دور specifies origin 'from afar' and رستم is the subject. چو شیر is a simile 'like a lion' that follows the name and modifies his manner. Word order is typical of Ferdowsi's elevated style with the simile placed after the subject.
segments.s418-g22: به here marks direction ('into' or 'toward'), زیر اندرون = 'under/inside' (literally 'beneath/within'), so به زیر اندرون expresses motion into the interior. اژدهای دلیر is a noun phrase 'the brave dragon' (دلیر modifies اژدهای). The whole phrase describes entering the dragon's interior — again the poetic order places directional phrase before the noun phrase that completes the image.
segments.s418-g23: چو آمد is a temporal subordinate ('when he came'). به نزدیک is a locative/directional phrase 'to/near' and اسفندیار is the proper name indicating whom he approached. The clause sets the time/place for what follows.
segments.s418-g24: هم‌انگه ('at that very moment') is an adverb. پذیره here denotes 'reception/acceptance' and شده + clitic ش (شدش) indicates that the action 'befell him' or 'he was received' — the clitic ش refers to the person being received (Rostam). نامدار is the agent/subject 'the famed one' placed after the verb in inverted poetic order: 'the famed one received him at that moment.'
segments.s418-g41: چو is the simile marker 'like/as' placed before the noun to introduce a comparison. کاموس here is a proper name (Kāmus) — the supplied senses don't include a proper-name option, so it is treated as a name in context. جنگی is a postpositive adjective meaning 'warlike' or 'fighter' modifying کاموس. The second چو repeats the simile; خاقان چین is a genitival phrase where چین (China) post-modifies خاقان (Khaghan), 'the Khaghan of China.'
segments.s418-g42: This is a nominal continuation of the simile: سواران (plural noun 'horsemen/riders') is modified by the postpositive adjective جنگی ('warlike'). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. مردان کین is a noun + postposed modifier ('men of kīn'), where کین is an attributive noun meaning 'vengeance/hatred', so 'men of vengeance.' The phrase has no finite verb and serves as an apposition to the preceding similes.
segments.s418-g43: که is a relative/introducing particle 'whom/that' linking this clause to what precedes. از پشت زینشان is a prepositional phrase 'from off the back(s) of their saddles' — زینشان is 'their saddle' (شان = possessive). به خم کمند is a motion phrase 'into the curve (bend) of the lasso' where به indicates movement toward/into and خم ('curve') modifies کمند ('lasso').
segments.s418-g44: ربودم is a past tense first-person verb 'I seized/snatched' (perfective). سر و پای is a coordinated object 'head and foot' (head and feet). کردم functions here as a secondary verb 'I made/caused' and with به بند ('into fetters') yields the idiom 'bound (them) in bonds' — i.e. 'I bound their heads and feet.' Word order places the verbs and coordinated objects in a compact, poetic sequence.
segments.s418-g45: نگهدار is a predicate noun 'guardian/preserver.' ایران و توران are the two objects/territorial names joined by و. منم is the emphatic copular form 'I am' placed after the predicate for emphasis and meter (typical Persian order: predicate + منم).
segments.s418-g46: به هر جای literally 'to/at every place' — here meaning 'wherever' or 'in every place.' پشت دلیران is a noun phrase where پشت carries the sense 'support/backing/protection' (not merely the physical back) and دلیران is 'the brave' (plural). منم repeats the emphatic 'I am,' giving the line its parallel structure and emphasis.
segments.s418-g47: ازین is the fused form of از این ('from/about this'); خواهش من is a genitive phrase 'my request/desire.' مشو is the negative imperative 'do not become' and when followed by بدگمان (an adjective) it means 'do not be suspicious/doubtful.' The clause politely asks the addressee not to mistrust what is being requested.
segments.s418-g48: مدان is an archaic/poetic negative imperative meaning roughly 'do not think/consider' (the provided senses mark it as obscure/archaic). خویشتن is the emphatic reflexive 'yourself.' برتر از آسمان is a comparative phrase 'superior to the sky/heavens' with از introducing the standard of comparison. The whole clause warns against imagining oneself above the heavens.
segments.s418-g9: برین is the fused prepositional phrase 'بر' + demonstrative 'این' meaning 'on this'; کوهه is the noun 'mountain' as the locatum; زین is the noun 'saddle' and is the thing being located on the mountain; که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; آهنست is 'آهن' + the archaic copula '-ست' = 'is (of) iron'. Together: 'On this mountain (is) a saddle which is of iron.'
segments.s418-g10: همان is a demonstrative 'that very/that'; رخش is 'steed/warhorse' (the referent); گویی here is the poetic particle 'as if / it seems' (not the conversational noun listed), used to introduce a comparison; که again introduces a relative clause; آهرمنست = 'آهرمن' + '-ست' = 'is Ahriman' (i.e. 'is like the demon Ahriman').
segments.s418-g11: اگر هم is the conditional 'if even/even if'; نبردش here functions as the notion of 'one who fights him' / 'his opponent' (the noun/phrase built on نبرد); بود is the past copula 'were/was' used in the protasis; ژنده پیل is a noun phrase 'a ragged/shabby elephant' (ژنده modifying پیل). The clause means 'Even if (his) opponent were a ragged elephant...'.
segments.s418-g12: برافشاند is a transitive verb 'to throw/ fling/ scatter' (here as the apodosis 'he would fling'); از is the preposition 'from'; تارک in this context names the 'nape/back' (an archaic/local sense not in the provided list) and thus takes از as the point 'from the nape of'; پیل is 'elephant'; نیل here likely qualifies the elephant ('Nile-elephant' or 'toward the Nile') — the whole means 'he would fling (it) from the elephant's nape (toward the Nile)'.
segments.s418-g13: کسی مَرد ... ندید is a negative existential statement: with ندید (did not see) and کسی (anyone) the sense is 'no one has seen'. ازین سان = 'of/from this kind/so' (از + این + سان) and به گیتی = 'in the world' (به used with 'گیتی' meaning 'in'). Word order places the object 'مرد' before the verb 'ندید' as typical in classical Persian.
segments.s418-g14: نه is a coordinating negative 'nor' (continuing the negation); از here means 'of/about' (indicating 'heard of'); نامداران پیشین = 'the earlier renowned ones'; شنید is the past 'heard' (i.e. 'have heard of it'). The clause parallels the previous negative: 'nor have the earlier famed ones heard (of such a thing)'.
segments.s418-g15: خرد نیست is an existential negative 'there is no wisdom' (خرد = wisdom, نیست = does not exist); اندر سر شهریار is a locative phrase 'in the head/mind of the king' (اندر = in, سر = head/mind as 'inner thought', شهریار = king). The clause states that such wisdom is absent from the king's mind.
segments.s418-g16: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that/which'; جوید is the third-person present 'seeks/desires'; ازین here is 'from/against this' (از + این) and names the object of seeking; نامور کارزار = 'renowned battle/renowned (one) of battle' — together the clause means 'to seek battle with this famed one' or 'to desire to fight this renowned warrior.'
segments.s418-g89: This is the topic clause: هنر (virtue/excellence) is the subject and باید (must/ought) marks what ought to be. از introduces the sources ('from'), and the three nouns مرد، فر، نژاد are listed in a parallel series (man/valour, farr/royal glory, lineage). Word order places the sources after باید for a poetic emphasis.
segments.s418-g90: This predicate describes qualities virtue 'should' bear. کفی (کفّی) 'a palm/hand' + راد 'generous/noble' = 'a generous hand' (an idiom for open-handedness); دارد is the present-tense verb 'has/possesses'. دلی پر ز داد literally 'a heart full of داد' — here داد is the abstract noun 'justice', and پر ز (poetic for پر از) means 'full of'. Note the poetic inversion (object/attributive phrase before verb).
segments.s418-g91: This is an archaic/poetic conditional with inverted order. سزاوار ... نیست جای expresses 'there is no place' for someone who is not سزاوار (worthy). گر (part of the conditional) and the pronominal clitic sequence ت + را form 'ترا' ('you' as object), but word-order is inverted for emphasis: literally, 'worthy-of-me, if you are not, place is not (for you)'.
segments.s418-g92: مرا هست is an idiomatic verbless/existential expression meaning 'for me there is' or 'my portion is'. هست is the copula/'there is', followed by a list of nouns پیروزی، هوش، رای (victory, intellect/wisdom, and رای as 'counsel/plan' in this context). The construction states what belongs to the speaker: 'my lot/role is ...'.
segments.s418-g93: ازان پس ('thereupon/after that') is an adverbial phrase fronted for narrative sequence. بفرمود is the polite/honorific past tense 'he said/commanded' (literary form). فرزند شاه is the direct object of that speech/command ('the king's son').
segments.s418-g94: که introduces the reported infinitival/subordinate clause: 'that (he) place the golden throne before the court.' کرسی زرین is the noun phrase 'the golden throne' (object) placed before the verb نهد, which here is a classical 3rd-person present/subjunctive/form of نهـاد meaning 'to place/set'. پیش گاه ('in the presence/ before the court') is a prepositional phrase giving location/occasion.
segments.s418-g95: بدان تا is a connective: ب + دان (here forming 'بدان' = 'to that/so that' or 'tell him') + تا ('so that/in order that'). گو here is the verb 'say/tell' (imperative/optative sense 'tell/command [so that]'). نامور پهلوان is the noun phrase 'the famed/renowned champion'. Together the phrase issues the instruction to call or order that champion.
segments.s418-g96: نشیند is the 3rd-person present/subjunctive of 'نشستن' used here as an optative/command 'let him sit' or 'may he sit'. بر introduces the locus 'upon/on', شهریار جوان is the compound 'the youthful king/sovereign' — idiomatically 'sit upon the young king' means to take the king's seat (i.e., sit on the throne of the young sovereign).
segments.s418-g81: This clause reports speech: جهاندیده (the speaker's epithet) + گفت (said) introduces the quoted content. این نه جای منست is a nominal clause meaning 'this is not my place' where جای (place) is predicated of این (this) and منست (an older/poetic copular/adjectival form meaning 'is fitting/appropriate' or 'is mine' depending on context) negated by نه before جای. Word order is Subject (جهاندیده) – Verb (گفت) – reported clause; منست is a poetic form used where Classical Persian would use است.
segments.s418-g82: This is a subordinate/continuation of the reported speech: بجایی نشینم is a first-person volitional/subjunctive-like form 'I will sit' or 'let me sit' (نشینم) with بجایی 'in an appropriate place'. که introduces a relative clause 'where/that' (here functioning as 'where') and رای منست 'is my counsel/opinion' — رای is the noun 'opinion/plan' and منست repeats the poetic copula/adjectival 'is fitting/belongs to me'. The sentence thus links intention (I will sit) to the condition/place matching his counsel.
segments.s418-g83: This is the reporting clause: تهمتن (the hero's name/epithet) + بفرمود, a polite/honorific past verb meaning 'he ordered/said'. کز is the fused form meaning 'that/from which' introducing the following locative phrase, and دست راست is a noun phrase 'the right hand' (دست = hand, راست = right). Word order is Name – verb – subordinate marker – locative.
segments.s418-g84: نشستی بیارای is a short clause giving the commanded action; here the morphs are somewhat irregular/poetic (نشستی appears in the text where a form of نشستن 'sit' is intended and بیارای relates to adornment). The final phrase ازان کم سزاست is an evaluative comment: ازان 'than that / from that', کم 'less / insufficient', and سزاست 'it is fitting/it is deserved' (here negative sense: being less is not fitting). Overall: 'sit at the right hand and be adorned; being less than that is unbecoming.'
segments.s418-g85: چنین 'thus' modifies گفت 'said' (again reported speech); با شاهزاده is the prepositional phrase 'to/with the prince' (با here functions as addressing/with), and به خشم is an adverbial phrase 'in anger' or 'angrily' (به + noun). Word order places the manner/adverbial after the verb phrase, a common Classical Persian pattern.
segments.s418-g86: This is the content of the speech introduced by که 'that/so that': آیین من بین is an imperative instruction 'observe/see my custom' where آیین 'custom/rite' is the object and بین is the imperative 'see!' or 'look!'. و links to a second imperative بگشای چشم 'open (بگشای) your eyes (چشم)' — بگشای is a command form. The construction stacks two imperatives joined by و.
segments.s418-g87: This is a paired imperative: هنر بین 'see/observe the skill' (هنر = skill/virtue, بین = imperative 'see') joined by و 'and' to the noun phrase این نامور گوهرم 'this famed jewel of mine' (این = this, نامور = renowned, گوهرم = 'my gem' where the pronominal suffix -م marks possession). The sequence is imperative verb + direct object and then a coordinated noun phrase.
segments.s418-g88: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that': از تخمه سام 'from the seed/stock of Sam' (از = from, تخمه = offspring/seed, سام = the proper name Sam) and کنداورم is a poetic/archaic verbal form related to 'to dig out / to take out' or a compound denoting production/bringing-forth with the 1st-person suffix -م. The clause qualifies 'this jewel of mine' by origin (from Sam's line).
segments.s418-g1: نشست is the past form of نشستن 'to sit/settle' used here as 'alighted/sat (down)'; its object/point of departure is marked by از بر رخش literally 'from upon Rakhsh' (preposition از + locative بر + name رخش). چون introduces a simile 'like', followed by پیل مست 'a drunken elephant' as the thing being compared to.
segments.s418-g2: This noun phrase describes what he held: یکی 'a certain/one' introduces the item; گرزه is the headword 'mace'; گاو پیکر is a compound attributive 'cow-bodied' or 'of ox-like bulk' (gaav modifying piker 'body'); به دست is an instrumental/locative phrase 'in (his) hand' with به marking the relationship and دست 'hand'.
segments.s418-g3: بیامد is past 'came'. دمان here is a concrete noun 'hem/slope/edge' (the landscape), the subject that 'came'; تا ... marks extent 'as far as / up to' and به نزدیک آب = 'near the water' (به + نزدیک + آب). The clause describes physical approach to the water.
segments.s418-g4: This clause says that the army was hurried to meet someone: سپه 'army/host' is marked as definite object by را; به دیدار او 'to meet him' (به + noun phrase) states the goal; بد شتاب is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning 'with ill/rapid haste' (بد modifying شتاب 'haste'), i.e. 'hurriedly'. The finite verb 'made/hastened' is implied by context and the clause functions as a predication about moving the army.
segments.s418-g5: هرانکس که is a free relative 'whoever that...'; از لشکر indicates the source/set 'from the army/host' (از + لشکر) and او را uses the object clitic را with او 'him' to mark the person seen; بدید is past 'saw'. Word order follows Persian relative clause pattern with the verb at the end of the clause.
segments.s418-g6: دلش is دل + enclitic possessive ش 'his heart/mind' as the subject; مهر و پیوند is a coordinated object 'love and bond' (مهر = affection, پیوند = link/bond); او is the target 'with him'; برگزید is the past tense 'chose/selected'. The verb governs the coordinated nouns as what the heart selects.
segments.s418-g7: همی is a literary particle indicating habitual or ongoing action ('used to / would'); گفت is the verb 'said' (here in the sense 'it was said / people said'); هرکس که این نامدار introduces a subordinate clause 'that whoever this famed one...' with نامدار functioning as 'this famous/renowned one'. The clause sets up the proverb-like statement that follows.
segments.s418-g8: نماند is the past of ماندن 'to remain' used here negatively in the sense 'was not left / was unmatched'; به کس جز 'to anyone except' is the idiom meaning 'no one except'; به سام سوار names the sole exception 'to Sam, the rider' (به + name + appositive سوار). The whole phrase states that no one could compare except Sam the rider.
segments.s418-g49: This is a prepositional/possessive phrase with the speaker as topic: من (I) + از بهر (a classical compound preposition meaning 'for the sake of' or 'because of') precedes the coordinated nouns این فر و اورند تو. The two nouns (این فر و اورند) are joined by و, and the pronoun تو placed after اورند functions as a possessive applying to the coordinated phrase by proximity (classical Persian often attaches the possessive to the final coordinated member).
segments.s418-g50: بجویم is a present/subjunctive form with the prefix بـ- marking ongoing/habitual or subjunctive aspect — here 'I seek' / 'I should seek'. همی is the archaic habitual particle meaning 'always/still' and follows the verb here for emphasis. رای و پیوند تو is the coordinated object (رای = 'counsel, opinion'; پیوند = 'bond/alliance'), with تو ('you') again marking possession of the coordination.
segments.s418-g51: نخواهم is the negative of خواستن in the first person — here 'I do not want / I will not have'. The conjunction که introduces the subordinate clause. چنان/چون تو یکی شهریار is a noun phrase meaning 'a king like you' (چون 'like/as' precedes the comparison), with یکی giving the sense 'one / a single'. Word order is typical classical Persian: comparison + addressee + numeral + noun.
segments.s418-g52: This clause gives the unwanted outcome. تبه here conveys 'misfortune / being ruined' (used adjectivally/nominally), and دارد is the present tense of داشتن 'to have' used idiomatically with روزگار ('fate') and a genitive/agent phrase to indicate being subject to fate. از چنگ من is a prepositional phrase 'from my grip/hand' (چنگ = 'claw/grip' used figuratively). Overall the clause means roughly 'be (or be made) ill-fated / be wrested by fate from my hand.'
segments.s418-g53: که introduces a content clause continuing the previous sentence. من is the subject; سام یل را is a marked object phrase with را showing definiteness/emphasis ('Sam, the hero'). بخوانم is the present/subjunctive form of خواندن used here in the sense 'to call/name or to summon' (contextually 'to call/name as'); دلیر follows as a predicate adjective modifying سام یل. The verb form fits the hortative/subjunctive sequence introduced by نخواهم که.
segments.s418-g54: کزو is a fused relativizing/prepositional pronoun meaning 'from whom / from which' and links back to سام/دلیر; بیشه is the locative 'thicket/grove'. بگذاشتی is the second-person past form 'you placed/left/let (something be)' (the b- prefix is part of classical past forms here). نره شیر is a noun phrase 'male lion' with the adjective نره ('male') preceding شیر. The clause reads as a relative/causal specification: 'from whom you left a male lion in the thicket.'
segments.s418-g55: This is a copular/predicative clause: به گیتی (lit. 'in the world') sets the location/sphere, منم is the copula-inflected pronoun 'I am'. ز (poetic form of از) marks origin/possession 'of him' (i.e. 'a memento of him'), کنون is the temporal adverb 'now', and یادگار is the predicate noun 'memento/memorial'. Word order is standard for Persian poetry: locative/time phrases can precede the main clause and the possessive z follows the verb phrase.
segments.s418-g56: This short nominal apposition names who the memento is: دگر 'another' modifies شاهزاده 'prince', then a secondary title/epithet یل 'hero' precedes the proper name اسفندیار. Classical Persian allows stacked epithets and appositions like this: 'another prince, the hero Esfandiyar.'
segments.s418-g73: به + noun phrase introduces the cause/occasion ('at the sight of / on seeing'); here 'به دیدار...' = 'at the sight/meeting of...'. 'دستان' is the plural 'hands' (literal, not 'tales'), and 'شوم' is an adjective 'ill‑omened / unlucky' modifying that noun phrase. 'شادمان' is a predicate adjective meaning 'joyful' and completes the clause (nominal, no explicit finite verb here — a common poetic compression: 'at the sight of the ill‑omened hands (I) became joyful').
segments.s418-g74: 'به تو' is a beneficiary/dative phrase 'for you'. The core predication is 'شاد دارم روان' — 'I keep / I make (my) soul/spirit happy': 'دارم' is the present indicative 'I have/keep' used as a verbal kernel, 'روان' = 'soul/spirit' is the object/thing kept joyful, and 'یک زمان' is an adverbial temporal phrase 'for a while / for a moment' modifying the whole. Word order (adj + دارم + noun) is a poetic, somewhat inverted ordering but regular in classical Persian.
segments.s418-g75: 'کنون' = 'now'. 'تو' is the 2nd‑singular subject. The sequence 'ب + دین' here functions as the fused prepositional/adverbial 'بدین' (classical 'with this / by this means'), followed by the object 'رنج' = 'suffering, hardship'. 'برداشتی' is 2nd singular past of برداشتن meaning 'you bore / you undertook / you took (upon yourself)' — so the clause means 'now you have borne this hardship.' Note that the source text splits 'بدین' into parts; grammatically treat them as the prepositional phrase 'with/ by this'.
segments.s418-g76: 'به دشت آمدی' is a simple past 2nd‑singular clause 'you came to the plain' ('به' marks the goal). 'خانه بگذاشتی' follows as a second past action: 'you left/put aside the house' — 'بگذاشتی' is past 2nd‑singular of گذاشتن used here in the sense 'you left/abandoned' or 'you set down (your) house'. The two verbs are coordinated in sequence (typical epic narration).
segments.s418-g77: 'به آرام بنشین' is an imperative: 'sit (yourself) in calmness / sit calmly' — 'به' marks manner here ('in / with'), 'آرام' = 'calm(ly)', 'بنشین' is the 2nd‑singular imperative 'sit down'. 'و بردار جام' continues with another imperative: 'and take the cup' ('بردار' imperative of برداشتن, 'جام' = 'cup').
segments.s418-g78: 'ز' here marks cause/reason ('because of / from'). 'تندی و تیزی' are two nouns in coordination: 'haste' (تندی) and 'sharpness/keenness' (تیزی). 'مبر' is the negative imperative 'do not take' (مَبُر), and 'هیچ نام' is the object 'any/any single name' ('هیچ' = no/any, 'نام' = name). The clause therefore warns: 'Because of haste or sharpness do not take/utter any name.'
segments.s418-g79: 'به دست چپ خویش' is a prepositional phrase 'on/at his left hand' — 'دست چپ' = 'left hand' and 'خویش' is the reflexive/possessive 'his own' (here written as خوی + ش). 'بر جای کرد' is a past tense verbal phrase 'he placed / set (it) in place' (بر + جای + کردن = 'to set/put upon the place'). The phrase order places the locative phrase before the verb, as is common.
segments.s418-g80: 'ز' here means 'for / on account of' (cause/beneficiary). 'رستم' is the name Rostam. 'همی' is a classical verbal particle (here marking the action as ongoing/habitual or simply as a poetic verbal auxiliary). 'مجلس آرای کرد' is the verb phrase 'he adorned / beautified the assembly' — 'مجلس' = assembly/gathering and 'آرای کردن' = 'to adorn/array'. Taken together: 'For Rostam he thus adorned the hall.'
segments.s418-g33: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily/indeed'. The preposition به introduces the object toward which the attitude is directed (به مردی = 'towards a man' or 'of a man'). مردی is indefinite 'a man' (representative of men). سبک داریم is a compound predication: سبک (here 'frivolous/light in worth') with دارم (present 'we have') yields 'we hold (him) light' = 'we treat (men) with little esteem.' Word order places the prepositional object before the predicate, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s418-g34: This is a parallel clause: two prepositional phrases (به رای و به دانش) mark the domains concerned ('in regard to counsel/opinion and in regard to learning/knowledge'). تنک داریم uses تنک ('thin/meagre') with دارم to express scarcity: 'we are meagre/poor in (our) counsel and learning.' The adjective precedes داریم and the repeated به keeps the parallel structure.
segments.s418-g35: به گیتی sets a locative frame 'in the world' (به + گیتی). چنان دان is an imperative/hortative 'consider/know thus' where دان functions as a 2nd‑person imperative 'know/consider'. که introduces the content clause 'that I am Rostam' (رستم منم) with the copula fused into منم 'I am.' The clause order (locative + adverb + verb + که + clause) is poetic but grammatically straightforward.
segments.s418-g36: فروزنده modifies تخم and functions as an epithet 'radiant/illuminating.' تخم ('seed, origin') is the head noun; نیرم here appears to be a possessive or genitive form related to 'strength/force' but its exact sense is not covered in the provided senses (see note). منم again is the copular predicate 'I am.' The phrase thus reads '[I am] the radiant seed/origin of (my) strength' — adjective + noun + possessor phrase followed by the copula.
segments.s418-g37: بخاید is an archaic/optative conditional form meaning roughly 'should (it) ...' or 'if ... were to'. ز من indicates the target 'on me' or 'from me' (i.e. 'seize me'); چنگ دیو سپید is the agentive noun phrase 'the claw of the white demon' (چنگ = 'claw, talon'; دیو = 'demon'; سپید = 'white'). Word order is poetic: the conditional verb appears before its agent phrase.
segments.s418-g38: بسی functions as a quantifier here ('much/many'—contextually 'many'). جادوان را is the definite object ('the magicians/wizards', with را marking the object). کنم is first‑person present/optative 'I will make / I shall cause', and ناامید is the result/complement 'hopeless/despairing.' Together: 'I shall make many magicians despair.'
segments.s418-g39: بزرگان is the subject 'the great ones/nobles'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'when/that', and دیدند is past 'they saw.' ببر مرا (here written in the line split as ببر + م + را) functions as the object 'my tiger' (ببر = 'tiger'; ـم + را = 'my' + object marker), so the clause reads 'when the nobles saw my tiger.' The segmentation in the manuscript splits مرا into م + را; that orthographic fragment (م) represents the pronominal element and is irregularly listed.
segments.s418-g40: همان is a demonstrative 'that very/same'; رخش is the proper name/term for a steed (Rakhsh, Rostam's horse) here meaning 'steed/warhorse'. غران 'roaring' is an epithet modifying رخش, and هژبر is an additional epithet or personal name (here treated as a name/epithet attached to the steed). The trailing م + را again represent the possessive/object clitic 'مرا' ('mine'/'my'), so the whole phrase says 'that same roaring Rakhsh, Hojbar, was mine.' The noun phrase precedes the copular sense implied by the context.
segments.s418-g57: بسی modifies پهلوان as an emphatic/adverbial word meaning 'much/very' (a poetic intensifier). پهلوان (noun) is the head of the noun phrase 'very/ many a hero'; جهان follows as a genitive/qualifying noun 'of the world'. بوده‌ام is the perfect/past participle of بودن with the first-person singular suffix, yielding 'I have been' (present perfect meaning 'I have been / I was'). The sequence Adverb + Noun + Qualifier + copula is a common poetic order to present a self-description that links directly to the next clause.
segments.s418-g58: سخنها (plural) is the topicalized object 'words/tales/speeches' and appears at the start for emphasis. ز هر گونه is a prepositional phrase ('from/of every kind') where ز is the poetic variant of از. بشنوده‌ام is the perfect first-person 'I have heard' and continues the same subject from the previous clause (the speaker), so the two clauses form a coordinated statement about the speaker's experience.
segments.s418-g59: سپاسم is a nominal expression 'my thanks' used elliptically to mean 'I thank' (common in poetic Persian). ز یزدان 'to/for God' uses ز as the poetic form of از/برای. که introduces a subordinate clause 'that', and بگذشت is the simple past third-person 'has passed' (time passed); سال is the subject 'a year'. The whole segment is 'I thank God that a year has passed.'
segments.s418-g60: بدیدم is past first-person 'I saw'. یکی before a noun here means 'a certain' (indefinite/introducing someone). The noun phrase شاه فرخ همال places the noun 'king' first, followed by epithets: فرخ 'fortunate' and همال an epithet meaning something like 'peer/one of similar rank' (an attributive noun in apposition). This is the speaker reporting having seen a particular king; adjectives follow the head noun in classical Persian.
segments.s418-g61: که here is the relative/introducing particle 'who/that' referring back to the king just mentioned. کین is the object 'vengeance' and خواهد is the modal/future auxiliary 'will' (third-person singular), so the verb complex means 'will take/exact vengeance'. از marks the target/agent of that vengeance 'from/on' and مرد ناپاک دین is a noun phrase 'the man impure in religion' = 'the impious man.' The clause means 'who will exact vengeance on the impious man.'
segments.s418-g62: جهانی here functions as an adverbial 'throughout the world' or 'worldwide'. برو بر together is a poetic combination where بر (= 'upon/over') links to کنند (third-person plural 'they do/will do'); the verb phrase برو بر کنند آفرین literally 'they will put acclamation upon' — idiomatically 'they will acclaim/cheer' (i.e. will cry 'bravo/praise') across the world. Note the older poetic word order places the adverbial and particles before the verb and its object.
segments.s418-g63: توی here is an archaic/poetic form used vocatively (equivalent to 'thou' / 'you are' or addressing someone directly) and introduces the description. نامور 'renowned' and پرهنر 'full of skill/learned' are adjectival epithets following the (vocative) head شهریار 'king/sovereign'. The order is typical of Persian epithets: (vocative) + epithets after the noun they qualify.
segments.s418-g64: به جنگ is a direction/goal phrase 'to/for battle'; اندرون is an adverbial 'into the midst/within'. افسر is the helmet (an old word for helmet) and کارزار is 'battle/field' qualifying the helmet as 'the war-helmet'. The sequence places the goal and inner-location expressions before the noun phrase 'war-helmet', so the whole says 'into the battle's midst he put on/donned the war-helmet.'
segments.s418-g25: بدو گفت رستم is lit. 'to him said Rostam' (بدو = 'to him' + گفت = 'said'), so the indirect object precedes the verb in this poetic word order. که‌ای پهلوان is a vocative: که‌ای here functions like classical 'که ای' = 'O' (addressing someone) followed by پهلوان 'champion'.
segments.s418-g26: A string of epithets in apposition to پهلوان: نوآیین 'newly come/new', نوساز 'newly-made/renewed', فرخ جوان 'fortunate youth'. The coordinating و simply joins the adjectives; this is a poetic catalogue of attributes addressed to the addressee.
segments.s418-g27: This phrase places an adverbial/nominal form خرابی ('proud stride' / 'with a proud gait') before the verb نیرزید (an archaic past‑form with negative sense), then the subject مهمان تو 'your guest'. The construction means roughly 'your guest did not display a proud gait' — note نیرزید is archaic/poetic and negates the action.
segments.s418-g28: چنین بود 'it was thus/so', followed by a temporal clause تا بود پیمان تو 'as long as your pact/pledge existed'. تا + past بود here gives 'while/as long as' (temporal), and پیمان تو is the noun phrase 'your covenant/pledge'.
segments.s418-g29: سخن هرچ گویم is a relative/conditional clause 'whatever words I say' (هرچ + verb گویم 'I say'); the verb here is first‑person (poetic/present/subjunctive). همه یاد گیر is an imperative phrase 'take/keep all in memory' (یاد 'memory' + گیر 'take/seize' forming a verbal idea 'remember').
segments.s418-g30: مشو تیز is a negative imperative 'do not become sharp/harsh'; با پیر 'with an elder' is a prepositional phrase; بر خیره خیر is a compact, inverted clause meaning roughly 'to stare/gape (خیره) is not good (خیر)' — بر here is a prepositional/intensifying particle used poetically. The whole warns against harshness and gaping at an old man.
segments.s418-g31: همی (an emphatic/continuous particle) + خویشتن را بزرگ آیدت: خویشتن را 'yourself' (را marks the object/topic) + بزرگ 'great' + آیدت 'it comes to you / it appears to you' — idiomatically 'you will consider yourself great' or 'you will feel great'. آیدت is used impersonally/experientially here.
segments.s418-g32: وزین 'weighty/dignified' qualifies نامداران 'the renowned/illustrious ones'; سترگ is a poetic adjective here (meaning large/great) and آیدت again 'comes to you' — together: 'a grave/weighty renown among the famed will befall/come to you.' Note سترگ is an archaism in this line.

sentences.s418-s49: He came and sat upon that golden throne; full of anger, he held a roundel in his hand.
sentences.s418-s33: Esfandiyar laughed at Rostam, and said to him, “O son of Sam, rider.”
sentences.s418-s34: You became narrow‑hearted when the proud strider did not come; I did not seek from these words either pleasure or renown.
sentences.s418-s35: I did not put you through such a bitter day and a long road; do not be quick to cause distress.
sentences.s418-s36: All morning I kept saying that at break of day I would offer a plea for pardon and take the road toward justice.
sentences.s418-s9: In this way, ever in pursuit of the crown and sometimes, he causes the famed one to be killed like the moon.
sentences.s418-s10: In old age he turns more toward treasure; he is more given to love and to rustic coquettes.
sentences.s418-s11: Rostam came from afar like a lion; into the dragon's belly he went, the brave one.
sentences.s418-s12: When he came near Esfandiyar, immediately the renowned one received him.
sentences.s418-s21: Like Kāmus the warrior, like the Khaghan of China — warlike horsemen and men of vengeance.
sentences.s418-s22: Whom I snatched from the backs of their saddles into the bend of my lasso, and I bound head and foot in fetters.
sentences.s418-s23: I am the guardian of Iran and Turan; in every place I am the backing of the brave.
sentences.s418-s24: Do not mistrust this request of mine; do not suppose yourself higher than the sky.
sentences.s418-s5: On this mountain there is a saddle of iron; that very steed seems as if it were Ahriman.
sentences.s418-s6: Even if his opponent were a ragged elephant, he would hurl it from the elephant's nape to the Nile (i.e. would throw it off the elephant).
sentences.s418-s7: No man in the world has seen the like of this, nor have the earlier renowned ones heard of it.
sentences.s418-s8: There is no wisdom in the king's mind to seek battle with this renowned one.
sentences.s418-s45: Virtue ought to proceed from man, farr (royal glory), and lineage; it should have a generous hand and a heart full of justice.
sentences.s418-s46: If you are not worthy of me there is no place for you; my portion is victory, intelligence, and counsel.
sentences.s418-s47: Thereupon he commanded the king's son to set the golden throne before the court.
sentences.s418-s48: Then (he said) to that end: 'Tell him to have the renowned champion sit upon the young sovereign.'
sentences.s418-s41: The worldly-wise man said, "This is not my place; I will sit in a place that accords with my counsel."
sentences.s418-s42: Tahmatan commanded that he sit at the right hand and be made splendid; being otherwise is unbecoming.
sentences.s418-s43: Thus he said angrily to the prince, 'Observe my custom and open your eyes.'
sentences.s418-s44: See my skill and this renowned jewel of mine, which I have taken from the stock of Sam.
sentences.s418-s1: He dismounted from Rakhsh like a drunken elephant, with an ox-sized mace in his hand.
sentences.s418-s2: The slope (hem) came up close to the water, and he hastened the army to meet him.
sentences.s418-s3: Whoever among his host saw him, his heart chose love and a bond with him.
sentences.s418-s4: They used to say that no one matched this famed man except Sam the rider.
sentences.s418-s25: For the sake of this farr and your renown, I always seek your counsel and alliance.
sentences.s418-s26: I would not have it that a single king like you be snatched by fate from my grasp.
sentences.s418-s27: that I should call Sam a brave hero — from whom you left a male lion in the thicket.
sentences.s418-s28: In the world I am now his memento — another prince, the hero Esfandiyar.
sentences.s418-s37: At the sight of the hands (that were) ominous I was made joyful; for you I keep my spirit happy for a while.
sentences.s418-s38: Now you have borne this hardship; you came into the plain and left your house.
sentences.s418-s39: Sit down quietly and take the cup; because of haste and sharpness do not take (utter) any name.
sentences.s418-s40: He placed (it) on his left hand; for Rostam he thus adorned the assembly.
sentences.s418-s17: Truly, we hold men lightly; in counsel and in learning we are meagre.
sentences.s418-s18: Know this in the world: I am Rostam; I am the radiant origin of (my) strength.
sentences.s418-s19: If the white demon's claw should seize me, I would make many magicians despair.
sentences.s418-s20: When the great men saw my tiger, that very Rakhsh — the roaring Hojbar — was mine.
sentences.s418-s29: I have been a great champion of the world; I have heard words/tales of every kind.
sentences.s418-s30: I thank God that a year has passed; I saw a certain fortunate king, a match (peer).
sentences.s418-s31: who will exact vengeance on the impious man and will cause acclamation throughout the world.
sentences.s418-s32: O famed, well-skilled sovereign; you donned the war-helmet into the midst of battle.
sentences.s418-s13: Rostam said to him, "O champion — newly arrived, newly made, fortunate youth!"
sentences.s418-s14: Your guest did not stride arrogantly; it was so as long as your covenant lasted.
sentences.s418-s15: Whatever I say, remember it all; do not be harsh with an elder and do not gape — that is not right.
sentences.s418-s16: You will always regard yourself as great, and a weighty renown among the famed will come to you.
